content:
Member-only story

7 min read
1 hour ago

--

Most developers learn closures as a JavaScript feature.
Senior developers eventually realize they’re really a way of thinking about state.
Every JavaScript developer has heard the textbook definition.
“A closure is a function together with its lexical environment.”
Technically correct.
Practically useless.
It explains what a closure is without explaining why it exists or why experienced engineers rely on it constantly.
That’s why closures remain one of the most misunderstood concepts in JavaScript. Developers memorize interview examples, solve a few coding challenges, and move on — never realizing closures quietly power nearly every modern framework, library, and application they write.
React Hooks.
Middleware.
Event handlers.
Debouncing.
Memoization.
Module privacy.
Async control flow.
Caching.
State management.
Almost all of them depend on closures.
The problem isn’t that closures are complicated.
